steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The noun phrase liquid compounds is followed by an appositive phrase naming hexafluorobenzene and benzene. This appositive identifies which specific compounds have those molar masses—it's essential identifying information.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
Identify the structure: "hexafluorobenzene (C₆F₆) and benzene (C₆H₆)" is a restrictive appositive — it identifies WHICH compounds (essential information; only two specific compounds are being discussed). Restrictive appositives take no commas. The phrase ends just before the next clause begins.
  • Choice A — "compounds, ... C₆H₆),": commas on both sides treat the names as non-essential, but they are essential to identify the compounds. ✗
  • Choice B — "compounds ... C₆H₆),": missing opening comma but trailing comma creates a mismatched, broken structure. ✗
  • Choice C — "compounds, ... C₆H₆)": opening comma without closing comma — same parenthetical-treatment error as A, plus mismatched. ✗
  • Choice D — "compounds ... C₆H₆)": no commas — correctly treats the names as restrictive (essential identification). ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
